7020 W DEER SPRINGS WY LAS VEGAS,
6450 SKY POINTE DR LAS VEGAS, NV
F/U Shoulder Arthritis, Bilateral evaluated on March 16, 2026
HPI: This is an 84 year old male who:
presents with bilateral shoulder arthritis, effusion. Patient is post left shoulder cortisone injection. Patient reports
some improvement since last office visit. Patient would like to discuss possible treatment options for the right
shoulder. Patient is currently taking Tylenol for pain as needed.
Inspection: No erythema or swelling. No gross deformity.
Palpation: Tenderness over glenohumeral joint lines bilaterally.
Right: Forward flexion 0-140°, abduction 0-120°, external rotation 0-60°.
Left: Forward flexion 0-140°, abduction 0-120°, external rotation 0-60°.
Pain and crepitus with active and passive ROM.
Strength: 5/5 rotator cuff strength with pain-limited effort.
Special Tests: Neer and Hawkins mildly positive secondary to degenerative changes. No instability.
located on the right shoulder joint.
The initial decision to perform this procedure was made after evaluation during this visit.
The risks, benefits and alternatives of bursal injection were discussed with the patient. Risks include infection,
bleeding, transient hyperglycemia, cartilage damage, and tissue atrophy at injection site. No certain guarantees
have been made, patients understand that responses can vary and multiple procedures may be necessary. The
patient was identified and timeout confirmed the correct site for the procedure. The patient was positioned
appropriately. The overlying skin was prepped with alcohol and Betadine Ultrasound guidance was performed at 5
Mhz with a linear probe. The use of direct ultrasound visualizations of the needle (rather than a palpation-guided
procedure) was required to ensure accurate needle and injection placement, in order for diagnostic specificity
when evaluating effectiveness of the injection, and for safety purposes to minimize risk of bleeding or injury to
nearby neurovascular structures. The sterile ultrasonic gel was applied, and the probe was placed on the
affected shoulder. The subacromial bursal space was visualized and the needle was introduced into the space. A
total of 1 injections on the right shoulder joint were treated with 2 ml of Triamcinolone acetonide, 40mg.
Anesthesia was acheived with 2 ml of 1% lidocaine without epinephrine.
Patient can resume activities as tolerated. Please contact the office if there is swelling, redness or pain at the
Musculoskeletal care: I advised the patient that most patients with shoulder arthritis can achieve some measure of
pain relief from nonsurgical options like medications, lifestyle modifications, exercise and physical therapy, and
use of supportive devices. Surgery is reserved for patients who have persistent symptoms despite adequate
Expectations: I counseled the patient about the natural history of shoulder arthritis which typically has
exacerbations and remissions. Most people report pain accompanied by loss of range of motion and occasionally
popping or clicking. Many people report that changes in the weather also affect the degree of pain from arthritis.
The shoulder joint does not heal on its own when there is arthritis. The rate of progression of arthritis varies from
person to person. With time, the symptoms of arthritis may get worse. For some, conservative management is not
satisfactory. These individuals should consider surgical options.
Contact office if shoulder pain worsens or if the joint becomes warm and accompanied by a fever.
OTC Medication: Recommend over the counter medication PRN
Impression: Bilateral shoulder arthritis, left shoulder effusion
Diagnosis discussed at length with patient all questions were answered. Right shoulder injected in the office
today. Patient will follow up in 3 months. Patient will call or return with any questions.
Electronically Signed By: Megan Bichsel, 03/19/2026 07:39 PM PDT
